highlights: serene expressions on these faces are meant to symbolize the omnipresence of the king and his protective gaze over the empire.  ...    visitors navigate through narrow corridors and steep staircases. At the center of this complex is the main shrine, which once housed a large statue of the Buddha. This central location within Angkor Thom and its layout signifies the temple’s importance as both a religious and political symbol.  ...    The design and symbolism of Bayon Temple reflect the cosmological and spiritual beliefs of the Khmer Empire. The central tower is considered to represent Mount Meru, the center of the universe in Hindu and Buddhist cosmology. Surrounding this central tower, the other towers and faces radiate outwards, symbolizing the divine omnipresence and  ...    The faces themselves have been subjects of much interpretation. title: The Bayon – The Temple of Faces | theraulaz.ch
highlights:
Expression of the king’s benevolent watchfulness over the cardinal directions.  ...    ## Religious Symbolism

The Bayon expresses a Buddhist cosmology centered on the king: - The faces’ orientation implies a divine presence in all directions, an omniscient and benevolent power. - The temple embodies a mandala of a balanced universe, with the king at its center. - The towers represent the thousand faces of compassion watching over the kingdom.  ...    After Jayavarman VII’s death, his successors reverted to Hinduism, then adopted Theravāda Buddhism. The Bayon was  ...    ### Conclusion The Bayon is simultaneously a temple, political monument, work of art, and stone mandala. highlights: Cambodia. This pyramid temple, a Mah?y?na site, marked the end of an ancient royal Khmer  ...    tower. Its dark interior once housed a large, naga-protected buddha. At its consecration, Jayavarman was symbolically joined to this buddha and imbued with a divine cast in the process. And at his death, the king's ashes would have been placed underneath this image, creating a certain conceptual kinship between the Bayon and a st?pa with its internal relics. The well-known guardian faces on the Bayon's fifty-two towers wear characteristic choker necklaces and originally stared straight ahead. But when many had their eyes recut to gaze downward, Avalokite?vara became their most likely new  ...    Although Buddhist, the Bayon followed tradition in its merging of regional or ancestral gods with Buddhist and Hindu deities. The complex itself is built on three levels that are intended to create a mountain-like structure, symbolically and visually linking the Bayon to Mt. Meru.
